title: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow

items.subtopic: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Growth and Development | AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: Prevalence and Genetic Basis of ADPKD
items.content: ADPKD is a common autosomal dominant disorder leading to kidney failure, affecting approximately 12 million people globally. It exhibits complete penetrance but variable expressivity across all ethnic groups with an estimated prevalence of 1:1000 to 1:400.
keyPoints: ADPKD affects ~12 million worldwide
Prevalence is 1:1000 to 1:400
~85% of cases due to PKD1 mutations, ~15% due to PKD2
Only half of patients are clinically diagnosed during their lifetime
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Growth and Development | AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Growth and Development | AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: Pathophysiology of ADPKD
items.content: ADPKD involves the formation of cysts due to cellular defects such as increased cell proliferation, fluid secretion, and abnormal extracellular matrix. Mutations in PKD1 and PKD2 disrupt polycystin-1 and -2 function, leading to aberrant calcium signaling and cAMP increase, promoting cyst growth.
keyPoints: Cysts form due to defects like increased cell proliferation and fluid secretion
PKD1 and PKD2 mutations lead to dysfunction in polycystin-1 (PC1) and -2 (PC2)
Loss of ciliary function leads to aberrant calcium signaling and increased cAMP
Increased cAMP promotes cyst growth via chloride and aquaporin channels
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Growth and Development | AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Growth and Development | AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: Genetic Complexity in ADPKD
items.content: ADPKD is genetically heterogeneous, with PKD1 on chromosome 16p13 and PKD2 on chromosome 4q21-q23. The complexity of the PKD1 gene, including its duplication, poses challenges for genetic analysis.
keyPoints: PKD1 localized to chromosome 16p13, PKD2 to chromosome 4q21-q23
~85% of ADPKD cases due to PKD1 mutations; ~15% due to PKD2
PKD1 gene has duplicated regions complicating genetic analysis
GANAB and DNAJB11 genes also implicated in a small number of families
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Growth and Development | AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 2)
items.title: Cystogenesis in ADPKD
items.content: In ADPKD, cysts develop due to clonal growth of cells with a 'second hit' mutation. This occurs when the normal allele of PKD1 or PKD2 is somatically mutated, and additional genetic or epigenetic events can accelerate cyst formation.
keyPoints: Cysts originate from clonal growth following a 'second hit' mutation in PKD1 or PKD2.
Partial loss of function of Pkd1 with a proliferative environment leads to cystogenesis.
A 'third hit', such as additional genetic events, can promote rapid cyst formation.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 2)
items.title: Clinical Manifestations of ADPKD
items.content: ADPKD presents with progressive bilateral renal cysts, often detected before age 30. Symptoms include flank pain (~60% of patients), hematuria (~40%), and recurrent infections. Hypertension is common, occurring before GFR reduction.
keyPoints: Focal renal cysts typically detected in individuals <30 years old.
Most patients have hundreds to thousands of cysts by their fifth decade.
~60% experience flank pain; ~40% have gross hematuria.
Infection is a leading cause of death, with up to 50% experiencing renal infections.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 2)
items.title: Complications and Management in ADPKD
items.content: ADPKD complications include hypertension, kidney stones (~20%), and cardiovascular issues as the major mortality cause. Hypertension often precedes GFR reduction and may involve increased renin-angiotensin activity.
keyPoints: Hypertension is common and typically occurs before reduced GFR.
~20% of patients develop kidney stones, with uric acid being predominant.
Cardiovascular complications are the major cause of mortality.
Increased activation of the renin-angiotensin system contributes to hypertension.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 3)
items.title: Diagnosis of ADPKD: Criteria and Imaging
items.content: ADPKD diagnosis relies on family history and renal cysts. Renal ultrasonography is used for presymptomatic screening, with specific criteria based on age to ensure accuracy.
keyPoints: Positive family history consistent with autosomal dominant inheritance and multiple kidney cysts bilaterally are typical diagnostic indicators.
For at-risk subjects aged 15-29 years: presence of at least two renal cysts (unilateral or bilateral) is sufficient for diagnosis, with a sensitivity value of 96% and specificity value of 100%.
Ages 30-59 years require at least two cysts in each kidney; ages ≥60 years require at least four cysts per kidney, both with sensitivity and specificity values of 100%.
CT scan and T2-MRI are more sensitive than ultrasonography but have limitations such as radiation exposure (CT) and minimal renal toxicity (T2-MRI).
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 3)
items.title: Intracranial Aneurysms in ADPKD: Risks and Screening
items.content: ADPKD patients have a higher risk of intracranial aneurysms, with specific considerations for screening based on family history.
keyPoints: Intracranial aneurysm (ICA) occurs four to five times more frequently in ADPKD patients than the general population and is associated with high mortality.
Family history of ICA is a risk factor for rupture; however, the role of hypertension and smoking as independent risk factors is unclear.
20–50% of patients may experience 'warning headaches' before subarachnoid hemorrhage due to ruptured ICA.
CT scan is typically used first for diagnosis, with lumbar puncture confirming it. The need for presymptomatic screening in asymptomatic patients remains unclear.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 3)
items.title: Extrarenal Complications of ADPKD
items.content: ADPKD is associated with several extrarenal complications, including liver cysts and valvular abnormalities.
keyPoints: Liver cysts are the most common extrarenal complication, particularly massive polycystic liver disease in women with multiple pregnancies.
Mitral valve prolapse occurs in up to 30% of ADPKD patients; other valvular issues include insufficiency of mitral, aortic, and tricuspid valves.
Increased prevalence of colonic diverticulae and abdominal wall hernias is noted in ADPKD patients.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease
items.title: Blood Pressure Management in ADPKD
items.content: Managing blood pressure is crucial for reducing cardiovascular complications and slowing renal disease progression in ADPKD. The JNC VIII report recommends a target of 140/90 mmHg, but more rigorous control (e.g., systolic BP to 110 mmHg) may increase the risk of renal disease progression by reducing renal blood flow.
keyPoints: Blood pressure target: 140/90 mmHg
More aggressive targets (systolic BP to 110 mmHg) can harm renal function in moderate or advanced ADPKD
Control via renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system inhibition
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease

items.subtopic: Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease
items.title: Treatment Approaches for Slowing Renal Progression
items.content: Several strategies target slowing renal disease progression in ADPKD by inhibiting cell proliferation and fluid secretion. These include mTOR pathway inhibitors (sirolimus, everolimus), V2R antagonists (OPC31260, tolvaptan), and somatostatin analogues. The HALT PKD trial showed that intensive renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system blockade could slow cyst growth.
keyPoints: mTOR inhibitors: sirolimus, everolimus
V2R antagonists: OPC31260, tolvaptan
Somatostatin analogues: octreotide-LAR
HALT PKD trial findings on intensive BP control and renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system blockade
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease

items.subtopic: Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease
items.title: Considerations for Dialysis and Transplantation in ADPKD
items.content: More than half of ADPKD patients will require peritoneal dialysis, hemodialysis, or kidney transplantation. Peritoneal dialysis may be unsuitable for those with massively enlarged kidneys due to limited intraabdominal space and increased risk of hernia and back pain. Pretransplant nephrectomy might be necessary in cases of recurrent renal cyst infection.
keyPoints: >50% of ADPKD patients require peritoneal dialysis, hemodialysis, or kidney transplantation
Peritoneal dialysis may not suit those with massively enlarged kidneys
Pretransplant nephrectomy for recurrent infections
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL RECESSIVE POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE
items.title: Genetic Basis of ARPKD
items.content: ARPKD is caused by mutations in the PKHD1 gene, which encodes fibrocystin/polyductin (FPC). This protein is involved in cell-cell and cell-matrix interactions and may regulate mechanosensory functions of primary cilia.
keyPoints: Prevalence: 1 in 20,000 live births
Carrier frequency: up to 1:70
Gene: PKHD1 on chromosome region 6p21.1–6p12.2
Transcript: encodes FPC, a large receptor-like integral membrane protein of 4074 amino acids
FPC localization: primary cilia of epithelial cells in renal and biliary systems
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL RECESSIVE POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL RECESSIVE POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE
items.title: Clinical Presentation and Prognosis of ARPKD
items.content: ARPKD is typically diagnosed in utero or neonatal period, presenting with enlarged kidneys. Early mortality is high due to respiratory issues; survivors often face renal insufficiency and systemic hypertension.
keyPoints: Diagnosis: In utero or within the neonatal period
Mortality: ~30% shortly after birth, 60% in first month
Common features: Renal insufficiency, ESRD, systemic hypertension
Liver involvement: Congenital hepatic fibrosis and Caroli disease
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL RECESSIVE POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL RECESSIVE POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE
items.title: Diagnostic Approach for ARPKD
items.content: Diagnosis of ARPKD can be made using ultrasonography, CT, or MRI. Ultrasonography is effective after 24 weeks gestation in severe cases.
keyPoints: Imaging: Ultrasonography reveals large, echogenic kidneys with poor corticomedullary differentiation
Diagnosis timing: After 24 weeks of gestation for severe cases
Distinguishing from ADPKD: Absence of renal cysts in parents >40 years old
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL RECESSIVE POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE

items.subtopic: Autosomal Recessive Polycystic Kidney Disease | TUBEROUS SCLEROSIS
items.title: Role of Tolvaptan in ARPKD Management
items.content: Tolvaptan, by inhibiting the vasopressin pathway through V2 receptor blockade, shows promise in reducing kidney cyst formation and preserving function in ARPKD. This suggests a potential therapeutic role despite the lack of specific therapies for ARPKD.
keyPoints: No specific therapy for ARPKD; supportive care includes neonatal intensive care, blood pressure control, dialysis, and kidney transplantation.
Tolvaptan inhibits vasopressin pathway via V2 receptor blockade.
Preclinical studies in PKHD1-mutated rat models show reduced cyst formation and preserved kidney function with tolvaptan.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · Autosomal Recessive Polycystic Kidney Disease | TUBEROUS SCLEROSIS

items.subtopic: Autosomal Recessive Polycystic Kidney Disease | TUBEROUS SCLEROSIS
items.title: Tuberous Sclerosis: Renal Manifestations and Management
items.content: TS, an autosomal dominant syndrome with renal manifestations like cysts and angiomyolipomas, requires regular imaging to monitor for complications such as renal cell carcinoma. Angiomyolipomas >4 cm often necessitate surgical intervention.
keyPoints: TS is caused by mutations in TSC1 or TSC2 genes; prevalence <1:5000 births.
Renal cysts occur in 20-30% of TS patients; angiomyolipomas are common and may require surgery if >4 cm.
Regular imaging recommended for renal cell carcinoma screening in TS with kidney involvement.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · Autosomal Recessive Polycystic Kidney Disease | TUBEROUS SCLEROSIS

items.subtopic: Autosomal Recessive Polycystic Kidney Disease | TUBEROUS SCLEROSIS
items.title: Mechanism and Treatment of Tuberous Sclerosis
items.content: The interaction between tuberin and hamartin inhibits mTOR signaling, leading to abnormal tissue growth. Everolimus, an mTOR inhibitor, is a first-line treatment for TSC-associated kidney tumors and other manifestations.
keyPoints: TSC1 and TSC2 gene products inhibit mTOR signaling, causing abnormal growth.
Everolimus approved for treating TSC-associated kidney tumors and nonkidney manifestations.
mTOR inhibitors are considered first-line treatment for TS.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · Autosomal Recessive Polycystic Kidney Disease | TUBEROUS SCLEROSIS

items.subtopic: VON HIPPEL-LINDAU DISEASE | OTHER INHERITED DISEASES OF TUBULE GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT
items.title: Recognizing Von Hippel-Lindau Disease in Patients with Kidney Cysts
items.content: Von Hippel-Lindau disease is an autosomal dominant condition caused by mutations in the VHL tumor-suppressor gene, leading to renal manifestations such as multiple bilateral kidney cysts and renal cell carcinomas. It should be considered in differential diagnosis for patients presenting with newly recognized kidney cysts.
keyPoints: Von Hippel-Lindau disease is autosomal dominant due to mutations in the VHL gene.
Kidney manifestations include multiple bilateral kidney cysts and renal cell carcinomas, affecting the majority of VHL patients.
Nonrenal features include pheochromocytomas, cerebellar hemangioblastomas, and retinal hemangiomas.
Annual screening with CT or MRI is recommended for early detection of renal cell carcinomas.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · VON HIPPEL-LINDAU DISEASE | OTHER INHERITED DISEASES OF TUBULE GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT

items.subtopic: VON HIPPEL-LINDAU DISEASE | OTHER INHERITED DISEASES OF TUBULE GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT
items.title: Approach to Management and Diagnosis of Inherited Tubule Disorders
items.content: Inherited disorders affecting tubules can lead to chronic kidney disease (CKD) without large cysts. DNA sequencing panels have improved the ability to diagnose these conditions rapidly and accurately, aiding in distinguishing between tubular and glomerular diseases.
keyPoints: Inherited tubule disorders can cause CKD and end-stage kidney disease without large cysts.
These disorders may lead to secondary glomerular stress and proteinuria or interstitial fibrosis.
DNA sequencing panels facilitate rapid and accurate genetic diagnosis of underlying causes, including diseases with cysts, tumors, and growth abnormalities.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · VON HIPPEL-LINDAU DISEASE | OTHER INHERITED DISEASES OF TUBULE GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT TUBULOINTERSTITIAL KIDNEY DISEASE (MEDULLARY CYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE)
items.title: Diagnosis and Genetic Basis of ADTKD-MUC1
items.content: ADTKD-MUC1, formerly known as MCKD I, is caused by mutations in the MUC1 gene. These mutations lead to a toxic 'neoprotein' fragment that affects kidney tubules, resulting in slowly progressive CKD in adulthood with minimal proteinuria and occasional renal cysts.
keyPoints: Mutations occur in the mucin 1 gene (MUC1).
No elevated uric acid levels unlike MCKD II.
Clinically presents as slowly progressive CKD, benign urine sediment, minimal proteinuria, and occasional renal cysts.
Histology shows tubulointerstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy.
Does not recur in transplanted kidneys.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT TUBULOINTERSTITIAL KIDNEY DISEASE (MEDULLARY CYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE)

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT TUBULOINTERSTITIAL KIDNEY DISEASE (MEDULLARY CYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE)
items.title: Clinical Features and Genetic Testing of ADTKD-UMOD
items.content: ADTKD-UMOD, also known as MCKD II, results from mutations in the UMOD gene. It is characterized by late-onset kidney disease with benign urine sediments, absence of significant proteinuria, and hyperuricemia. Genetic testing confirms diagnosis due to nonspecific clinical features.
keyPoints: Caused by mutations in the UMOD gene encoding uromodulin.
Clinically suspected with family history of late-onset kidney disease, benign urine sediments, absence of significant proteinuria, and hyperuricemia.
Associated conditions include familial juvenile hyperuricemic nephropathy (HNFJ1) and glomerulocystic kidney disease (GCKD).
Common noncoding sequence variants in UMOD are associated with increased risk of CKD.
Diagnosis confirmed by genetic testing.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT TUBULOINTERSTITIAL KIDNEY DISEASE (MEDULLARY CYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE)

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT TUBULOINTERSTITIAL KIDNEY DISEASE (MEDULLARY CYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE)
items.title: Management of ADTKD with Hyperuricemia and Gout
items.content: Patients with ADTKD, particularly those with UMOD or REN mutations, presenting with hyperuricemia and gout should be managed with uric acid-lowering agents like allopurinol or febuxostat.
keyPoints: ADTKD patients with hyperuricemia and gout treated with uric acid-lowering agents.
Common treatments include allopurinol or febuxostat.
Management approach is similar regardless of specific genetic mutation (UMOD or REN).
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT TUBULOINTERSTITIAL KIDNEY DISEASE (MEDULLARY CYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE)

items.subtopic: NEPHRONOPHTHISIS
items.title: Understanding the Genetic Basis and Clinical Course of Nephronophthisis (NPHP)
items.content: Nephronophthises, or nephronophthisis-related ciliopathies, are autosomal recessive disorders characterized by ciliary dysfunction. Over 90 genes have been identified that lead to these conditions, with mutations in the NPHP2 gene being a common cause of the juvenile form. These disorders often result in end-stage kidney disease (ESKD) and can present as isolated kidney issues or part of multi-organ syndromes.
keyPoints: Nephronophthisis is the most common inherited childhood form of kidney failure requiring replacement therapy.
>90 genes identified lead to NPHP under an autosomal recessive pattern.
Juvenile form often caused by mutations in the NPHP2 gene.
End-stage kidney disease typically develops in early adulthood for adolescent forms.
NPHP can be part of syndromes like Senior-Loken syndrome (with retinitis pigmentosa) and Joubert syndrome (with cerebellar vermis hypoplasia).
No specific clinical tests; genetic diagnosis is recommended.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · NEPHRONOPHTHISIS

items.subtopic: NEPHRONOPHTHISIS
items.title: Clinical Management of Nephronophthisis: Focus on Dialysis and Transplantation
items.content: Management of nephronophthisis primarily involves addressing the signs and systemic abnormalities associated with the disease, as there are no specific therapies. Most individuals affected by NPHP will eventually require chronic dialysis or kidney transplantation due to progressive renal failure.
keyPoints: Therapy is symptomatic; no specific treatments for NPHP.
Chronic dialysis or kidney transplantation is required for most patients with NPHP.
Proteinuria in NPHP is absent or mild, and urine sediment is not active.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · NEPHRONOPHTHISIS

items.subtopic: Karyomegalic Tubulointerstitial Nephritis
items.title: Understanding the Genetic Basis and Pathology of Karyomegalic Tubulointerstitial Nephritis
items.content: Karyomegalic tubulointerstitial nephritis is a rare, recessive genetic disorder leading to progressive kidney failure in adults. It results from inheriting two mutant copies of the FAN1 gene, which plays a crucial role in DNA repair mechanisms. The pathology includes chronic tubulointerstitial nephritis and interstitial fibrosis, with characteristic karyomegaly observed on kidney biopsy.
keyPoints: Exceptionally rare form of kidney disease
Adult-onset progressive kidney failure
Recessive disorder due to two mutant copies of the FAN1 gene
FAN1 is involved in DNA repair machinery
Kidney histology shows karyomegaly, interstitial fibrosis, and tubular atrophy
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · KARYOMEGALIC TUBULOINTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS | MEDULLARY SPONGE KIDNEY | CONGENITAL ABNORMALITIES OF THE KIDNEY AND URINARY TRACT

items.subtopic: Medullary Sponge Kidney
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Medullary Sponge Kidney
items.content: Medullary sponge kidney (MSK) is a benign condition often discovered incidentally. It involves developmental malformation and cystic dilatation of the renal collecting ducts, leading to variable-sized medullary cysts. MSK increases the risk of calcium phosphate and oxalate stone formation due to altered tubule flow characteristics. Patients may experience reduced kidney concentrating ability and a higher incidence of urinary tract infections.
keyPoints: Benign entity often diagnosed incidentally
Sporadic finding, not typically inherited
Caused by developmental malformation of renal collecting ducts
Associated with increased frequency of calcium phosphate and oxalate kidney stones
Patients may have reduced concentrating ability and more UTIs
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · KARYOMEGALIC TUBULOINTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS | MEDULLARY SPONGE KIDNEY | CONGENITAL ABNORMALITIES OF THE KIDNEY AND URINARY TRACT

items.subtopic: MITOCHONDRIAL DISEASE | DIAGNOSTIC CONSIDERATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.title: Mitochondrial Disease and Kidney Dysfunction: Recognizing Renal Phenotypes
items.content: Mitochondrial diseases, due to defects in oxidative phosphorylation, can affect multiple organs including the kidneys. The renal phenotype often involves tubulointerstitial disease, progression to kidney failure, glomerular involvement manifesting as proteinuria and glomerulosclerosis, and proximal tubule dysfunction such as Fanconi syndrome.
keyPoints: Mitochondrial genome defects commonly affect kidney function.
Tubulointerstitial disease may lead to kidney failure.
Proximal tubule activity changes are common, including Fanconi syndrome.
Patients may present with acidosis, hypophosphatemic rickets, hypercalciuria, glycosuria, and decreased urine concentrating ability.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · MITOCHONDRIAL DISEASE | DIAGNOSTIC CONSIDERATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: MITOCHONDRIAL DISEASE | DIAGNOSTIC CONSIDERATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.title: Genetic Testing in CKD: The Role of Large Panels or Whole Genome Sequencing
items.content: Recent advances suggest that genetic variants contribute significantly to chronic kidney disease (CKD) cases, even without a clear Mendelian phenotype. Rare genetically mediated kidney diseases often mimic each other phenotypically, supporting the use of large genetic panels or whole genome sequencing for accurate diagnosis.
keyPoints: Genetic testing is crucial in CKD due to significant contributions from Mendelian gene variants.
Rare kidney diseases can be difficult to categorize by phenotype alone.
Large genetic panels or whole genome sequencing are recommended for precise diagnosis.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · MITOCHONDRIAL DISEASE | DIAGNOSTIC CONSIDERATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Growth and Development | AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: What is the function of polycystin-2 (PC2) in ADPKD?
options: It functions as a G protein-coupled receptor.
It is a calcium-permeable six-transmembrane protein.
It regulates chloride and aquaporin channels directly.
It acts as an adenylyl cyclase inhibitor.
items.explanation: PC2 is a calcium-permeable six-transmembrane protein that structurally belongs to the transient receptor potential (TRP) cation channel family.

items.subtopic: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Growth and Development | AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: What is the role of PC1/2 protein complex in ADPKD?
options: It serves as a mechanosensor or chemical sensor.
It regulates only the actin cytoskeleton.
It functions solely as an adenylyl cyclase.
items.explanation: The PC1/2 protein complex serves as a mechanosensor or chemical sensor and regulates calcium and G-protein signaling.

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 2)
items.question: What is the primary genetic mechanism by which cysts develop in ADPKD?
options: Germline mutation of PKD1 or PKD2 alone
Somatic 'second hit' mutation in the normal allele of PKD1 or PKD2
Mutations in GANAB and DNAJB11 genes
Increased activation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system
items.explanation: Cysts develop from clonal growth due to a somatic 'second hit' mutation in the normal allele of PKD1 or PKD2.

items.question: Which symptom is frequently observed in approximately 60% of patients with ADPKD?
items.explanation: Back or flank pain is a frequent symptom in ~60% of patients with ADPKD.

items.question: What type of kidney stones are more common in patients with ADPKD compared to the general population?

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 3)
items.question: What is the most common extrarenal complication of ADPKD?
items.explanation: Liver cysts derived from the biliary epithelia are the most common extrarenal complication.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 3)
items.question: Which imaging modality is generally used as the first diagnostic test for intracranial aneurysm in ADPKD patients?
items.explanation: A CT scan is generally used as the first diagnostic test for intracranial aneurysm.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 3)
items.question: For at-risk subjects aged 30–59 years, what is required for the diagnosis of ADPKD?
options: At least two renal cysts bilaterally
At least four cysts in each kidney
Absence of at least two cysts in each kidney
items.explanation: The presence of at least two cysts in each kidney and at least four cysts in each kidney is required for diagnosis among at-risk subjects aged 30–59 years.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease
items.question: What is the recommended target blood pressure for reducing cardiovascular complications and renal disease progression in ADPKD according to JNC VIII guidelines?
items.explanation: The recommended target blood pressure is 140/90 mmHg according to the JNC VIII guidelines.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease

items.subtopic: Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease
items.question: Which antibiotic class is preferred for treating cyst infections in ADPKD due to their ability to penetrate cyst walls?
items.explanation: Lipid-soluble antibiotics are preferred for treating cyst infections in ADPKD because they can penetrate the cyst walls.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease

items.subtopic: Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease
items.question: Which trial evaluated the impact of intensive blockade of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system on progressive renal disease?
items.explanation: The HALT PKD trial evaluated the impact of intensive blockade of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system on progressive renal disease.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease

items.subtopic: Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease
items.question: Which treatment has been indicated by the FDA for patients at risk of rapidly progressing ADPKD?
items.explanation: The FDA has indicated tolvaptan for use in patients at risk of rapidly progressing ADPKD.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL RECESSIVE POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE
items.question: Which gene mutation is responsible for all clinical presentations of Autosomal Recessive Polycystic Kidney Disease (ARPKD)?
items.explanation: Mutations in the PKHD1 gene are responsible for all clinical presentations of ARPKD.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL RECESSIVE POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL RECESSIVE POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE
items.question: Where is the PKHD1 gene located on human chromosomes?
items.explanation: The PKHD1 gene is localized on human chromosome region 6p21.1–6p12.2.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL RECESSIVE POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL RECESSIVE POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE
items.question: What is the estimated prevalence of Autosomal Recessive Polycystic Kidney Disease (ARPKD) in live births?
items.explanation: The estimated prevalence of ARPKD is 1 in 20,000 live births.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL RECESSIVE POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL RECESSIVE POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE
items.question: What protein does the largest transcript of PKHD1 encode?
items.explanation: The largest transcript of PKHD1 encodes fibrocystin/polyductin (FPC).
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL RECESSIVE POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE

items.subtopic: Autosomal Recessive Polycystic Kidney Disease | TUBEROUS SCLEROSIS
items.question: Which drug is approved to inhibit the vasopressin pathway in ARPKD by blocking the V2 receptor?
items.explanation: Tolvaptan functions by inhibiting the vasopressin pathway through blockade of the vasopressin receptor 2 (V2).
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · Autosomal Recessive Polycystic Kidney Disease | TUBEROUS SCLEROSIS

items.subtopic: Autosomal Recessive Polycystic Kidney Disease | TUBEROUS SCLEROSIS
items.question: What is a common kidney finding in patients with tuberous sclerosis?
items.explanation: The most common kidney finding in TS is the presence of angiomyolipomas.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · Autosomal Recessive Polycystic Kidney Disease | TUBEROUS SCLEROSIS

items.subtopic: Autosomal Recessive Polycystic Kidney Disease | TUBEROUS SCLEROSIS
items.question: What percentage of people with tuberous sclerosis have renal cysts?
items.explanation: Renal cysts are observed in about 20–30% of people with TS.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · Autosomal Recessive Polycystic Kidney Disease | TUBEROUS SCLEROSIS

items.subtopic: Autosomal Recessive Polycystic Kidney Disease | TUBEROUS SCLEROSIS
items.question: Which gene is adjacent to PKD1 and can cause manifestations of both ADPKD and TS when deleted?
items.explanation: The TSC2 gene is adjacent to PKD1 in the human genome, and deletions can cause manifestations of both ADPKD and TS.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · Autosomal Recessive Polycystic Kidney Disease | TUBEROUS SCLEROSIS

items.subtopic: VON HIPPEL-LINDAU DISEASE | OTHER INHERITED DISEASES OF TUBULE GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT
items.question: Which of the following is a recommended screening method for early detection of renal cell carcinomas in patients with Von Hippel-Lindau disease?
options: Annual imaging with CT or MRI scanning
items.explanation: Annual screening of the kidneys by imaging with CT or MRI scanning is recommended for early detection of renal cell carcinomas in VHL patients.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · VON HIPPEL-LINDAU DISEASE | OTHER INHERITED DISEASES OF TUBULE GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT

items.subtopic: VON HIPPEL-LINDAU DISEASE | OTHER INHERITED DISEASES OF TUBULE GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT
items.question: What type of inheritance pattern is associated with Von Hippel-Lindau disease?
items.explanation: Von Hippel-Lindau disease is an autosomal dominant condition caused by mutations in the VHL tumor-suppressor gene.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · VON HIPPEL-LINDAU DISEASE | OTHER INHERITED DISEASES OF TUBULE GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT

items.subtopic: VON HIPPEL-LINDAU DISEASE | OTHER INHERITED DISEASES OF TUBULE GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a nonrenal feature of Von Hippel-Lindau disease?
items.explanation: Nonrenal features of VHL include pheochromocytomas, cerebellar hemangioblastomas, and retinal hemangiomas; polycystic liver disease is not mentioned.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · VON HIPPEL-LINDAU DISEASE | OTHER INHERITED DISEASES OF TUBULE GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT TUBULOINTERSTITIAL KIDNEY DISEASE (MEDULLARY CYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE)
items.question: Which gene is mutated in patients with medullary cystic kidney disease type I (MCKD I)?
items.explanation: Patients with MCKD I have mutations in the mucin 1 gene (MUC1).
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT TUBULOINTERSTITIAL KIDNEY DISEASE (MEDULLARY CYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE)

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT TUBULOINTERSTITIAL KIDNEY DISEASE (MEDULLARY CYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE)
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a common feature of MCKD II?
items.explanation: Despite the name, kidney cysts are not a common feature of MCKD II.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT TUBULOINTERSTITIAL KIDNEY DISEASE (MEDULLARY CYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE)

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT TUBULOINTERSTITIAL KIDNEY DISEASE (MEDULLARY CYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE)
items.question: What is the suggested new name for MCKD II and related UMOD-associated diseases?
items.explanation: The term uromodulin-associated kidney disease (UAKD) has been suggested as a better name for MCKD II and related UMOD-associated diseases.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT TUBULOINTERSTITIAL KIDNEY DISEASE (MEDULLARY CYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE)

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT TUBULOINTERSTITIAL KIDNEY DISEASE (MEDULLARY CYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE)
items.question: Which gene mutation is associated with ADTKD-REN?
items.explanation: ADTKD-REN patients have disease-segregating mutations in the renin gene (REN).
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT TUBULOINTERSTITIAL KIDNEY DISEASE (MEDULLARY CYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE)

items.subtopic: NEPHRONOPHTHISIS
items.question: Which gene mutation is most frequently associated with the juvenile form of nephronophthisis?
items.explanation: The juvenile form of nephronophthisis is usually caused by mutations in the NPHP2 gene.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · NEPHRONOPHTHISIS

items.subtopic: NEPHRONOPHTHISIS
items.question: What is a common feature shared among various forms of nephronophthisis?
items.explanation: Various forms of nephronophthisis share common features including tubulointerstitial fibrosis.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · NEPHRONOPHTHISIS

items.subtopic: NEPHRONOPHTHISIS
items.question: Which syndrome is characterized by the presence of nephronophthisis and retinitis pigmentosa?
items.explanation: Senior-Loken syndrome is defined by the presence of nephronophthisis with retinitis pigmentosa.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · NEPHRONOPHTHISIS

items.subtopic: KARYOMEGALIC TUBULOINTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS | MEDULLARY SPONGE KIDNEY | CONGENITAL ABNORMALITIES OF THE KIDNEY AND URINARY TRACT
items.question: Which gene mutation is associated with karyomegalic tubulointerstitial nephritis?
items.explanation: Karyomegalic tubulointerstitial nephritis is caused by mutations in the FAN1 gene.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · KARYOMEGALIC TUBULOINTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS | MEDULLARY SPONGE KIDNEY | CONGENITAL ABNORMALITIES OF THE KIDNEY AND URINARY TRACT

items.question: What imaging modality is less sensitive for detecting medullary sponge kidney (MSK) compared to intravenous pyelography?
items.explanation: CT urography is not as sensitive in detecting MSK compared to intravenous pyelography.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · KARYOMEGALIC TUBULOINTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS | MEDULLARY SPONGE KIDNEY | CONGENITAL ABNORMALITIES OF THE KIDNEY AND URINARY TRACT

items.question: Which of the following is a common association with medullary sponge kidney (MSK)?
options: Increased frequency of urinary tract infections
items.explanation: MSK is associated with an increased frequency of urinary tract infections.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · KARYOMEGALIC TUBULOINTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS | MEDULLARY SPONGE KIDNEY | CONGENITAL ABNORMALITIES OF THE KIDNEY AND URINARY TRACT

items.subtopic: MITOCHONDRIAL DISEASE | DIAGNOSTIC CONSIDERATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.question: Which of the following is a common renal phenotype associated with mitochondrial disease?
options: Glomerular involvement manifesting as proteinuria and glomerulosclerosis.
Isolated distal tubule dysfunction without other systemic manifestations.
Complete absence of any kidney-related symptoms.
Chronic pyelonephritis due to recurrent urinary tract infections.
items.explanation: Glomerular involvement, manifest as proteinuria and glomerulosclerosis, is a common renal phenotype in mitochondrial disease.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · MITOCHONDRIAL DISEASE | DIAGNOSTIC CONSIDERATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: MITOCHONDRIAL DISEASE | DIAGNOSTIC CONSIDERATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.question: What recent advancement has influenced the diagnostic approach to chronic kidney disease (CKD)?
options: The use of traditional biochemical tests alone.
New DNA sequencing technologies suggesting genetic variants contribute significantly.
Exclusive reliance on family history for diagnosis.
Abandonment of genetic testing in favor of symptomatic treatment.
items.explanation: Recent studies using new DNA sequencing technologies suggest that genetic variants contribute to a nontrivial fraction of CKD cases.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · MITOCHONDRIAL DISEASE | DIAGNOSTIC CONSIDERATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: MITOCHONDRIAL DISEASE | DIAGNOSTIC CONSIDERATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.question: What is the primary suspected cause of Mesoamerican nephropathy?
options: Toxic environmental factors and heat stress.
Genetic mutations in the mitochondrial genome.
items.explanation: Mesoamerican nephropathy is thought to be caused by a combination of toxic environmental factors and heat stress.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · MITOCHONDRIAL DISEASE | DIAGNOSTIC CONSIDERATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.statement: ADPKD is inherited as an autosomal recessive trait.
items.explanation: ADPKD is inherited as an autosomal dominant trait with complete penetrance but variable expressivity.

items.statement: PKD2 mutations are often underdiagnosed due to milder clinical disease.
items.explanation: Patients with PKD2 mutations may be >15% because they tend to have milder clinical disease and, as a result, are underdiagnosed.

items.statement: Renal cell carcinoma is more common in patients with ADPKD compared to the general population.
items.explanation: Renal cell carcinoma is a rare complication of ADPKD and does not occur at an increased frequency compared to the general population.

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 2)
items.statement: Proteinuria is usually a major feature of ADPKD.
items.explanation: Proteinuria is usually a minor feature of ADPKD.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 2)

items.statement: Hypertension and cigarette smoking are confirmed independent risk factors for aneurysm rupture in ADPKD.
items.explanation: Whether hypertension and cigarette smoking are independent risk factors is not clear.

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 3)
items.statement: Genetic testing for ADPK1 mutations is straightforward due to the small size of the gene.
items.explanation: Mutational analysis of the PKD1 gene is difficult and costly because of its large size and presence of pseudogenes.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease
items.statement: More rigorous blood pressure control is associated with greater clinical benefits in ADPKD.
items.explanation: More rigorous blood pressure control does not equal greater clinical benefits and may increase the risk of renal disease progression.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease

items.subtopic: Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease
items.statement: Peritoneal dialysis is suitable for all patients with ADPKD.
items.explanation: Peritoneal dialysis may not be suitable for some patients with massively enlarged polycystic kidneys.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL RECESSIVE POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE
items.statement: Most patients with ARPKD are compound heterozygotes for PKHD1 mutations.
items.explanation: Most patients with ARPKD have two different mutations in the PKHD1 gene, making them compound heterozygotes.

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL RECESSIVE POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE
items.statement: Macrocysts are common at birth in patients with ARPKD.
items.explanation: Macrocysts generally are not common at birth in ARPKD patients.

items.statement: The majority of TS-causing mutations are found in the TSC1 gene.
items.explanation: The majority of TS-causing mutations are actually found in the TSC2 gene.

items.statement: Tolvaptan is used to treat tuberous sclerosis-associated kidney tumors.

items.statement: ADPKD is the most common adult-onset single-gene form of kidney disease.

items.statement: Genetic testing for CKD should consider large panels or whole genome sequencing.
items.explanation: Rare genetically mediated kidney diseases may mimic each other, suggesting the use of large genetic panels in testing.

items.subtopic: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Growth and Development | AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 1)
items.assertion: Loss of ciliary function in PC1 and PC2 leads to aberrant calcium signaling.
items.reason: This results in increased adenylyl cyclase activity and decreased phosphodiesterase activity, causing elevated cellular cAMP.
items.explanation: The loss of ciliary function of PC1 and PC2 indeed leads to aberrant calcium signaling, which increases adenylyl cyclase activity and decreases phosphodiesterase activity.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Growth and Development | AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Growth and Development | AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 1)
items.assertion: PKD2 mutations account for the majority of ADPKD cases.
items.reason: Mutations in PKD2 are responsible for ~85% of ADPKD cases.
items.explanation: Mutations in PKD2 are responsible for ~15% of ADPKD cases, not the majority.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Growth and Development | AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 2)
items.assertion: Hypertension in ADPKD may result from increased activation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system.
items.reason: Increased sympathetic nerve activity and impaired endothelial cilium function-dependent relaxation contribute to hypertension in ADPKD.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 2)
items.assertion: Mutations in GANAB and DNAJB11 genes result in classic ADPKD.
items.reason: These mutations lead to milder cystic kidney disease with small renal cysts and normal-sized kidneys.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; these mutations result in milder forms of the disease, not classic ADPKD.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 3)
items.assertion: Polycystic liver disease associated with ADPKD does not progress to renal failure.
items.reason: ADPLD, which is caused by mutations in PRKCH and SEC63, does not progress to renal failure.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason pertains to a different condition (ADPLD) than ADPKD.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 3)
items.assertion: The presence of at least two renal cysts is sufficient for diagnosis among subjects aged 15–29 years.
items.reason: This criterion has a sensitivity value of 96% and specificity value of 100%.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease
items.assertion: Tolvaptan has been shown to reduce renal pain in ADPKD.
items.reason: Tolvaptan reduces cAMP levels by antagonizing the activation of vasopressin V2 receptor (V2R) in collecting ducts.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reduction of renal pain is not directly explained by the mechanism involving V2R.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease

items.subtopic: Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease
items.assertion: Combining different growth inhibitors may enhance efficacy and reduce side effects in ADPKD treatment.
items.reason: Targeting multiple pathways involved in cyst development can provide a synergistic effect.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL RECESSIVE POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE
items.assertion: ARPKD can be diagnosed in utero after 24 weeks of gestation.
items.reason: Severe cases of ARPKD show large, echogenic kidneys with poor corticomedullary differentiation on ultrasonography.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why ARPKD can be diagnosed in utero.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL RECESSIVE POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL RECESSIVE POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE
items.assertion: The lack of mutational hot spots makes molecular diagnosis of ARPKD straightforward.
items.reason: The large and complex genomic structure of PKHD1 complicates the molecular diagnosis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, while the reason is true; the complexity of PKHD1 makes molecular diagnosis difficult.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL RECESSIVE POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE

items.subtopic: Autosomal Recessive Polycystic Kidney Disease | TUBEROUS SCLEROSIS
items.assertion: Patients with tuberous sclerosis may require nephrectomy.
items.reason: TS-related cysts can become large and hemorrhagic, occasionally necessitating nephrectomy when nephron-sparing surgery is not possible.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why nephrectomy may be required.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · Autosomal Recessive Polycystic Kidney Disease | TUBEROUS SCLEROSIS

items.subtopic: Autosomal Recessive Polycystic Kidney Disease | TUBEROUS SCLEROSIS
items.assertion: Regular periodic imaging is recommended for TS patients with kidney involvement.
items.reason: This screening helps detect the development of renal cell carcinoma in these patients.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why regular imaging is recommended.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · Autosomal Recessive Polycystic Kidney Disease | TUBEROUS SCLEROSIS

items.subtopic: VON HIPPEL-LINDAU DISEASE | OTHER INHERITED DISEASES OF TUBULE GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT
items.assertion: Inherited diseases affecting the tubulointerstitial compartment of the kidney can lead to secondary glomerular stress and glomerulosclerosis.
items.reason: Disorders of glomerular function will typically lead to secondary interstitial fibrosis and tubule atrophy.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion as they describe different pathways.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · VON HIPPEL-LINDAU DISEASE | OTHER INHERITED DISEASES OF TUBULE GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT TUBULOINTERSTITIAL KIDNEY DISEASE (MEDULLARY CYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE)
items.assertion: ADTKD-MUC1 patients have elevated uric acid levels.
items.reason: MUC1 mutations alter a highly repetitive region within the gene, leading to toxic effects on kidney tubules.
items.explanation: In contrast to MCKD II patients, individuals with MCKD I do not have elevated uric acid levels.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT TUBULOINTERSTITIAL KIDNEY DISEASE (MEDULLARY CYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE)

items.subtopic: AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT TUBULOINTERSTITIAL KIDNEY DISEASE (MEDULLARY CYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE)
items.assertion: Genetic testing is the definitive method for diagnosing specific forms of ADTKD.
items.reason: Kidney biopsies show interstitial fibrosis, which are not diagnostic of any particular genetic entity.
items.explanation: The specific diagnosis must be made by other means, such as genetic testing.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT TUBULOINTERSTITIAL KIDNEY DISEASE (MEDULLARY CYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE)

items.subtopic: KARYOMEGALIC TUBULOINTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS | MEDULLARY SPONGE KIDNEY | CONGENITAL ABNORMALITIES OF THE KIDNEY AND URINARY TRACT
items.assertion: CAKUT is a major contributor to kidney failure in children.
items.reason: It accounts for more than one-third of end-stage kidney disease in this group.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders of Tubule Grow · KARYOMEGALIC TUBULOINTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS | MEDULLARY SPONGE KIDNEY | CONGENITAL ABNORMALITIES OF THE KIDNEY AND URINARY TRACT
